Gas: Short-range: Two-particle (binary) collisions are the rule.
Plasma: Long-range: Collective motion of particles is ubiquitous in plasma, resulting in various waves and other types of collective phenomena.

Gas: Very low: Gases are excellent insulators up to electric field strengths of tens of kilovolts per centimetre.[27]
Plasma: Very high: For many purposes, the conductivity of a plasma may be treated as infinite.

Gas: One: All gas particles behave in a similar way, largely influenced by collisions with one another and by gravity.
Plasma: Two or more: Electrons and ions possess different charges and vastly different masses, so that they behave differently in many circumstances, with various types of plasma-specific waves and instabilities emerging as a result.
